stem: What are the last two digits of \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \)? (That is, the value of \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \bmod 100 \).)
solution: The last two digits are \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \bmod 100 \). Since \( \gcd([[a]], 100) = 1 \), Euler's theorem gives \( [[a]]^{40} \equiv 1 \pmod{100} \); reducing the exponent \( [[b]] \) modulo 40 yields the value \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), one power too many.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-1 \), one power too few.
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod 100 \), not the value at exponent \( [[b]] \).
This differs from the correct last two digits by 1.
This differs from the correct last two digits by 10 (a tens-digit slip).
This differs from the correct last two digits by 11.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod 100 \), the base itself.

stem: The base \( [[a]] \) shares a common factor with 100, so Euler's theorem does not apply directly. What are the last two digits of \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \)?
solution: Since \( [[a]] \) is even, \( \gcd([[a]],100)\ne 1 \) and Euler's theorem cannot be applied to the whole modulus directly. Instead reduce mod 4 and mod 25 separately (or note the power sequence mod 100 becomes eventually periodic once enough factors of 2 accumulate) and recombine: \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{100} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), one power too many.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-1 \), one power too few.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-2 \), two powers too few.
This differs from the correct last two digits by 1.
This differs from the correct last two digits by 10 (a tens-digit slip).
This differs from the correct last two digits by 10 in the other direction.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod 100 \), the base itself, as if \( [[b]]=1 \).

stem: What are the last two digits of \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \cdot [[c]]^{[[d]]} \)?
solution: Reduce each factor's last two digits separately using Euler's theorem (\( [[a]]^{40}\equiv 1 \), \( [[c]]^{40}\equiv 1 \pmod{100} \)), then multiply the residues and reduce mod 100 once more: \( [[a]]^{[[b]]}\cdot[[c]]^{[[d]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{100} \).
distractor_rationales: This only reduces the first factor, dropping the second entirely.
This only reduces the second factor, dropping the first entirely.
This adds the two reduced factors instead of multiplying them.
This differs from the correct last two digits by 1.
This differs from the correct last two digits by 10 (a tens-digit slip).
This differs from the correct last two digits by 10 in the other direction.
This uses one extra power of the second factor.

stem: The base \( [[a]] \) is a multiple of 5, so \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) is automatically divisible by 25; combining that with \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \bmod 4 \) via the Chinese Remainder Theorem pins down the last two digits exactly. What are the last two digits of \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \)?
solution: Since \( [[a]] \) is a multiple of 5 and \( [[b]] \ge 2 \), \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) is a multiple of 25, so the last two digits lie in \( \{00, 25, 50, 75\} \); checking \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \bmod 4 \) selects which one. Combining via CRT gives \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{100} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), one power too many, flipping which multiple of 25 it lands on.
This multiplies the base by the exponent instead of exponentiating.
This adds the base and exponent instead of exponentiating.
This uses \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod 100 \), as if the exponent were 2.
This is one more than the correct last two digits.
This is one less than the correct last two digits.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod 100 \), the base itself, as if \( [[b]]=1 \).

stem: What are the last two digits of \( [[a]]^{[[b]]^{[[c]]}} \)? (The exponent itself is \( [[b]]^{[[c]]} = [[expo]] \); reduce THAT exponent modulo 40 using Euler's theorem to finish.)
solution: First evaluate the tower exponent itself: \( [[b]]^{[[c]]} = [[expo]] \). Then, since \( \gcd([[a]],100)=1 \), Euler's theorem reduces \( [[expo]] \) modulo \( \varphi(100)=40 \), giving \( [[a]]^{[[expo]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{100} \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( [[b]] \) and \( [[c]] \) instead of raising \( [[b]] \) to the power \( [[c]] \).
This uses one more than the correct tower exponent.
This uses one less than the correct tower exponent.
This uses only \( [[b]] \) as the exponent, dropping \( [[c]] \) entirely.
This differs from the correct last two digits by 10 (a tens-digit slip).
This differs from the correct last two digits by 11.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod 100 \), the base itself, as if the tower exponent were 1.
